response: the environmental scientist is studying the population dynamics of an endangered species of turtle on a remote island. the turtle population is modeled by a logistic growth model with the following differential equation: dP/dt = rP(1 - P/K) - h(t), where P(t) is the population of turtles at time t, r is the intrinsic growth rate of the population, K is the carrying capacity of the environment, and h(t) is a harvesting function representing the number of turtles lost due to environmental threats each year. the intrinsic growth rate r = 0.1 per year, the carrying capacity K = 1000 turtles, and the harvesting function is modeled by h(t) = 10 + 5sin(π/6 t). determine the equilibrium population size P_e of the turtles where the population remains constant over time. the equilibrium population size is found by setting dP/dt = 0, so 0 = rP(1 - P/K) - h(t). solving for P, we get P = 0 or P = K(1 - h(t)/rP). the equilibrium population is the value of P where the population remains constant over time, so P_e = K(1 - h(t)/rP). the value of P_e is 1000(1 - (10 + 5sin(π/6 t))/(0.1P)). the equilibrium population size is 1000(1 - (10 + 5sin(π/6 t))/(0.1P)). the equilibrium population size is 1000(1 - (10 + 5sin(π/6 t))/(0.1P)). the equilibrium population size is 1000(1 - (10 + 5sin(π/6 t))/(0.1P)). the equilibrium population size is 1000(1 - (10 + 5sin(π/6 t))/(0.1P)). the equilibrium population size is 1000(1 - (10 + 5sin(π/6 t))/(0.1P)). the equilibrium population size is 1000(1 - (10 + 5sin(π/6 t))/(0.1P)). the equilibrium population size is 1000(1 - (10 + 5sin(π/6 t))/(0.1P)). the equilibrium population size is
